_notes: Tier-1: the 16 Round-of-16 teams. FIFA ranks from the 11 June 2026 ranking where verified; null where not yet verified.
group letters and group-stage records not yet verified — group_summary carries the verified Round-of-32 result instead.

teams.name: Canada
teams.group_summary: Beat South Africa 1-0 in the Round of 32 — the first men's World Cup knockout win in Canadian history — before falling 0-3 to Morocco in the Round of 16.
teams.best_wc_finish: Round of 16 (2026) — already the deepest run in their history
teams.wc_history: Canada qualified for the men's World Cup just twice before co-hosting this one — in 1986, when they left without scoring, and 2022, when they scored their first-ever goals but went home after the groups. Everything happening now is brand new for them.
teams.storyline: The co-hosts are in completely uncharted territory: their first knockout stage, powered by a golden generation that grew up dreaming of exactly this. A raucous home crowd and two genuine world-class attackers make them a dangerous underdog.
teams.style: Fast and direct — they want to win the ball and sprint at you before your defense is set.
fun_facts: Canada is one of three co-hosts of this World Cup, along with Mexico and the USA.
Their Round-of-32 win over South Africa was the first men's World Cup knockout victory in the country's history.

teams.name: Morocco
teams.group_summary: Beat the Netherlands on penalties (1-1, 3-2 pens) in the Round of 32.
teams.best_wc_finish: Fourth place (2022) — the first African team ever to reach a World Cup semifinal
teams.wc_history: Morocco made history in 2022 as the first African and first Arab nation to reach a World Cup semifinal, knocking out Spain and Portugal along the way. They've carried that momentum into a 7th-place world ranking — their highest ever.
teams.storyline: The 2022 fairytale wasn't a fluke, and Morocco are here to prove it. They've already eliminated the Netherlands on penalties — the same script that sank Spain four years ago — and a second straight deep run would cement them as a genuine world power.
teams.style: Disciplined and patient: a compact defense that absorbs pressure, then breaks at speed through their flying fullbacks.
fun_facts: Morocco's 7th place in the FIFA rankings is the highest in the country's history.
They have now won World Cup penalty shootouts against both Spain (2022) and the Netherlands (2026).

teams.name: Paraguay
teams.group_summary: Stunned Germany in the Round of 32 — 1-1 after extra time, 4-3 on penalties.
teams.wc_history: A proud footballing nation that reached the 2010 quarterfinals, Paraguay then missed three straight World Cups before roaring back for this one. They've never lacked fight — their identity is defense, grit, and making favorites miserable.
teams.storyline: Paraguay just produced the upset of the tournament, knocking out Germany on penalties after Julio Enciso's opener and a heroic rearguard effort. Nobody wants to play them now — least of all a France side that must break down the tournament's most stubborn defense.
teams.style: A deep, disciplined block that concedes almost nothing, plus quick counters and total commitment to every tackle.
fun_facts: Their shootout win over Germany was widely called one of the greatest upsets in World Cup history.
This is Paraguay's first World Cup since 2010 — and they've already matched a generation's best result.

teams.name: France
teams.group_summary: Beat Sweden 3-0 in the Round of 32, with a Kylian Mbappé brace.
teams.wc_history: Two-time champions and the dominant tournament team of the modern era: winners in 2018, finalists in 2022 (losing an all-time final to Argentina on penalties), and semifinalists or better in four of the last five World Cups.
teams.storyline: France are doing what France do: gliding through knockout rounds while everyone else sweats. Mbappé has six goals and the Golden Boot in his sights, and the supporting cast — led by reigning Ballon d'Or winner Ousmane Dembélé — might be the deepest squad in the field.
teams.style: Total control without the ball and devastating pace on the break — they're happy to let you have possession and then kill you in three passes.
fun_facts: France have reached at least the semifinal in four of the last five World Cups.
The 2022 final they lost to Argentina on penalties is widely called the greatest World Cup final ever played.

teams.name: Brazil
teams.group_summary: Beat Japan 2-1 in the Round of 32.
teams.wc_history: The most successful nation in World Cup history with five titles — but the last one came in 2002, an eternity by Brazilian standards. Every tournament since has ended in tears against European opposition.
teams.storyline: Five stars on the shirt and a 24-year itch. Under Carlo Ancelotti — the first foreign coach to lead Brazil at a World Cup — they've ditched some of the chaos for structure, while Vinícius Júnior supplies the magic. A knockout draw through Norway and then Mexico or England looks navigable... which is exactly when Brazil usually stumble.
teams.style: Structured and pragmatic by Brazilian standards, but with front players who can produce a piece of skill from nowhere.
fun_facts: Brazil are the only country to have played in every single World Cup.
Their five titles are the most of any nation — but none since 2002.

teams.name: Norway
teams.group_summary: Beat Ivory Coast 2-1 in the Round of 32.
teams.wc_history: Norway hadn't been to a World Cup since 1998 — a 28-year wait that ended the moment Erling Haaland's generation came of age. Their last appearance included a famous group-stage win over Brazil, whom they've never lost to.
teams.storyline: The longest-suffering fans at this tournament finally have a team worth the wait: Haaland, arguably the world's best pure goalscorer, and Martin Ødegaard, one of its classiest playmakers, leading Norway's first knockout campaign since 1998 — straight into a rematch with Brazil.
teams.style: Compact defending and rapid vertical attacks — everything is designed to get the ball to Haaland in the box as fast as possible.
fun_facts: Norway have played Brazil four times and never lost — including a 2-1 win at the 1998 World Cup.
This is their first World Cup appearance in 28 years.

teams.name: Mexico
teams.group_summary: Beat Ecuador 2-0 in the Round of 32.
teams.wc_history: Mexico are World Cup regulars with a curse: for decades they kept reaching the Round of 16 and losing — a national obsession known as the quest for 'el quinto partido', the fifth game. Their only two quarterfinals came when they hosted, in 1970 and 1986.
teams.storyline: Host nation, home crowd, and the ghost they've chased for forty years standing right in front of them: win one knockout game at the Estadio Azteca and Mexico finally play 'el quinto partido' again. Standing in the way — England. No pressure.
teams.style: Energetic and aggressive with the crowd behind them — high pressing, quick combinations, and waves of attacks in bursts.
fun_facts: The Estadio Azteca is the first stadium ever to host matches at three different World Cups (1970, 1986, 2026).
Both of Mexico's World Cup quarterfinals came on home soil — exactly the situation they're in now.

teams.name: England
teams.group_summary: Beat DR Congo 2-1 in the Round of 32.
teams.wc_history: One World Cup title, won at home in 1966, followed by six decades of near-misses that torment the country every two summers. Recent teams have come agonizingly close: a semifinal in 2018 and back-to-back European Championship finals.
teams.storyline: England always believe it's coming home, but this squad has a harder edge under Thomas Tuchel, and in Harry Kane — five goals so far — the most reliable scorer in the knockout rounds. First, though: a World Cup night at the Azteca against the host nation. Welcome to the deep end.
teams.style: Patient control and world-class set pieces, built on a defense that rarely gives up big chances.
fun_facts: England have never won a World Cup knockout match on Mexican soil — they went out in the 1970 quarterfinal, their only previous knockout game there.
Harry Kane won the Golden Boot at the 2018 World Cup and is chasing another.

teams.name: United States
teams.group_summary: Beat Bosnia and Herzegovina 2-0 in the Round of 32 — the USA's first World Cup knockout win since 2002.
teams.wc_history: The USA reached the semifinals of the very first World Cup in 1930 and a quarterfinal in 2002, but have mostly been a plucky Round-of-16 team since. This generation — almost all playing at top European clubs — was built for a home World Cup.
teams.storyline: The hosts just exorcised a 24-year demon with their first knockout win since 2002, on goals from Folarin Balogun and a Malik Tillman thunderbolt. Now comes the revenge game: Belgium, who broke American hearts in extra time in 2014 — and the USA must do it without the suspended Balogun.
teams.style: High-energy pressing and athleticism — they want the game fast, physical and loud.
fun_facts: The USA's third-place finish in 1930 remains their best-ever World Cup result.
Striker Folarin Balogun scored against Bosnia, then was sent off in a controversial call — so he misses the Belgium rematch.

teams.name: Belgium
teams.group_summary: Survived Senegal 3-2 in the Round of 32, coming from two goals down and winning with a controversial late penalty.
teams.wc_history: Belgium's 'golden generation' — De Bruyne, Lukaku and company — spent a decade ranked among the world's best, peaking with third place in 2018. They've been chasing the trophy that generation was promised ever since, and time is nearly up.
teams.storyline: Written off before the tournament, Belgium's aging stars produced the comeback of the round: two goals down against Senegal, they roared back to win 3-2 with a hotly disputed late penalty. Now Kevin De Bruyne, 35, gets one more shot at the country that celebrated beating Belgium's babies in 2014 — except that time, Belgium won.
teams.style: Slower and craftier than their peak years — patient possession, then one perfect through-ball from De Bruyne.
fun_facts: Belgium spent much of 2018-2022 ranked No. 1 in the world without winning a major trophy.
Their 2014 Round-of-16 win over the USA — 2-1 in extra time — is the last time these teams met at a World Cup.

teams.name: Spain
teams.group_summary: Beat Austria 3-0 in the Round of 32 — their first World Cup knockout win since lifting the 2010 trophy.
teams.wc_history: Spain won it all in 2010 with the greatest passing team ever assembled, then spent three World Cups falling on penalties or to counterattacks. The 2024 European champions arrived here as many people's favorites — and just ended a 16-year knockout drought emphatically.
teams.storyline: The best pure footballing side in the world finally looks like it again at a World Cup: Mikel Oyarzabal's double dismantled Austria 3-0, teenage sensation Lamine Yamal is running the show, and now comes the derby — Portugal, and quite possibly Cristiano Ronaldo's last stand, with a quarterfinal on the line.
teams.style: Endless short passing that starves you of the ball, then sudden killer moves through the teenage wingers.
fun_facts: The win over Austria was Spain's first World Cup knockout victory since the 2010 final itself.
Lamine Yamal turns 19 during this tournament — he was already a European champion at 17.

teams.name: Portugal
teams.group_summary: Beat Croatia 2-1 in the Round of 32 — a Ronaldo penalty, then a Gonçalo Ramos winner four minutes into stoppage time.
teams.wc_history: For all their stars, Portugal have never reached a World Cup final — their best remains third place in 1966. The Ronaldo era brought a European title (2016) and two Nations Leagues, but the big one is the hole in the trophy cabinet.
teams.storyline: Cristiano Ronaldo is 41, playing a record sixth World Cup, and refusing to leave quietly: his penalty and Gonçalo Ramos's 94th-minute header just broke Croatia. Around him, a genuinely elite midfield led by Vitinha does the modern work. Next: Spain, the neighbor and the nemesis, in a knockout for the first time ever.
teams.style: A world-class midfield controls the tempo; the attack mixes young legs with the oldest killer instinct in the sport.
fun_facts: Ronaldo and Messi are both appearing in a record sixth World Cup this summer.
The last World Cup meeting with Spain was 2018's epic 3-3 draw — Ronaldo scored a hat-trick.

teams.name: Argentina
teams.group_summary: Survived a huge scare against Cape Verde in the Round of 32, winning 3-2 in extra time.
teams.wc_history: Three-time champions and the reigning holders after the epic 2022 final win over France — the night Lionel Messi completed football. They arrived in North America ranked No. 1 and chasing something not done since 1962: back-to-back World Cups.
teams.storyline: The champions are alive, but barely: minnows Cape Verde took them to extra time before falling 3-2. Messi, 39, leads the tournament with seven goals and every game is another 'last World Cup match of his career' watch. Next up, a storyline for the ages — Messi vs Salah.
teams.style: Everything flows through Messi's brain: slow, suffocating control with sudden bursts of brilliance, plus a nasty streak of grit when needed.
fun_facts: No nation has won back-to-back World Cups since Brazil in 1958 and 1962.
Messi's seven goals lead the Golden Boot race at age 39.

teams.name: Egypt
teams.group_summary: Beat Australia 4-2 on penalties (1-1 after extra time) in the Round of 32 — Egypt's first-ever World Cup knockout win.
teams.best_wc_finish: Round of 16 (2026) — the deepest run in their history
teams.wc_history: Egypt were the first African nation ever to play at a World Cup, back in 1934 — yet they had never won a knockout match until this week. The Pharaohs are the record African champions but the World Cup had always ended early for them.
teams.storyline: Ninety-two years after their World Cup debut, Egypt finally have a knockout win — Emam Ashour's header, a nerveless shootout, and national delirium. Their reward is the hardest job in football: stopping Messi's Argentina, with Mo Salah carrying the hopes of a continent.
teams.style: Organized, physical defending in a deep block, with everything in attack funneled through Salah's counterattacks.
fun_facts: Egypt were the first African and first Middle Eastern nation to play at a World Cup (1934).
The shootout win over Australia was the first knockout victory in their World Cup history.

teams.name: Switzerland
teams.group_summary: Beat Algeria 2-0 in the Round of 32 — Switzerland's first World Cup knockout win in 88 years.
teams.wc_history: Switzerland qualify for almost everything and had lost every World Cup knockout match since 1938 — a run of heartbreak that finally ended this week. Their last quarterfinal came in 1954, when they hosted the tournament.
teams.storyline: Eighty-eight years of knockout misery ended in one night against Algeria, with Breel Embolo and Dan Ndoye scoring. Now captain Granit Xhaka leads a tidy, veteran side one win away from a first quarterfinal in 72 years — with Argentina or Egypt likely waiting after that.
teams.style: Compact, disciplined and deeply organized — they control games through midfield and almost never beat themselves.
fun_facts: Before this week, Switzerland hadn't won a World Cup knockout match since 1938.
They famously eliminated France on penalties at Euro 2020 — this core knows how to topple giants.

teams.name: Colombia
teams.group_summary: Beat Ghana 1-0 in the Round of 32, with an early Jhon Arias goal.
teams.wc_history: Colombia's best World Cup was 2014, when James Rodríguez lit up Brazil and won the Golden Boot as they reached the quarterfinals. After missing 2022 entirely, this team rebuilt around Luis Díaz's speed and reached a Copa América final in 2024.
teams.storyline: Colombia don't blow teams away — they squeeze them, as Ghana found out in a 1-0 vice grip. With Luis Díaz in world-class form and James Rodríguez conducting one last World Cup, they're quietly two wins from the first semifinal in the country's history.
teams.style: Tough, streetwise and rhythmic — long spells of control, and one moment of Díaz acceleration to decide it.
fun_facts: James Rodríguez won the 2014 Golden Boot, including the goal of the tournament against Uruguay.
Colombia have never reached a World Cup semifinal — it's the great national 'what if'.
